text: The surgeon begins addressing an incarcerated right indirect inguinal hernia, starting dissection at the medial umbilical ligament and progressing laterally towards Bogros' space. The peritoneal flap appears thinner, typical in younger patients.
The surgeon carefully maneuvers around the infraepigastric vessel, maintaining a thin peritoneal flap while pushing fat laterally over the nerves.

The surgeon uses a Maryland dissector to take down the flap in a standard fashion, with the option of using a force bipolar for larger or thickened sacs. The procedure is primarily performed with monopolar scissors, a Maryland dissector, and a camera.
The surgeon enters the loose areolar plane within the supravesicular space, preparing to cross over to adjacent anatomical areas.
I really like a good medial dissection because I like the flap to be large enough to accommodate an extra large barred three D max, which is why I always use.
The surgeon pulls the indirect hernia sac medially while applying cautery, gently sweeping the sac away from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon observes the spermatic cord falling away, revealing a small cord lipoma. The hernia sac is exposed, and nearby vessels begin to come into view.
The surgeon pulls up on the hernia sac and applies cautery, causing the tissue to peel away smoothly.
The surgeon maintains a position medial to the vas deferens, ensuring careful dissection in this area.
The surgeon continues the medial dissection, carefully working on the tissue surrounding the hernia sac to create a large flap.
The surgeon pulls up on the indirect hernia sac medially, causing the vas deferens to fall laterally, visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon pulls up on the hernia sac and applies cautery, causing the tissue to peel away and revealing underlying structures.

The camera is positioned at an oblique angle as the surgeon continues dissection around the corner, pulling up the flap. The surgeon ensures to reach below the iliac down to the obturator fossa, preparing the area for the mesh to lay flat.
The surgeon identifies the chronomortis vein located to the right side of the surgical field.
The surgeon continues dissection around the corner, removing tissue medial to the vas deferens, which is visible falling laterally.
The surgeon visualizes the deferential vessels and carefully maneuvers around the corner, ensuring the vessels remain intact during the procedure.
The surgeon lays the lateral dissection plane wide to ensure the mesh will lay flat without buckling during closure.
Again, extra large barred, three max.
The surgeon begins suturing at the site of the indirect hernia, focusing on lateral fixation to ensure the mesh lays flat and secure.
The surgeon drapes the mesh across the iliac vessels, positioning it around the corner, and fixates it laterally on Cooper's ligament, just medial to the iliac vessels.
The surgeon performs two fixations to secure the mesh, ensuring it lays smoothly over the surgical area, followed by a high medial fixation.
The surgeon demonstrates the complete separation of the flaps from the mesh, showing a clear distinction between the two structures.
The surgeon desufflates the surgical area, allowing the mesh to lay smoothly and securely in its intended position.
The surgeon snugly secures the superior aspect of the mesh to prevent its migration upwards, ensuring it remains in the desired position.
I almost never leave any gaps. Some people leave gaps immediately here just to allow for the air and the CO₂ to have an exit point.
The surgeon closes the flap while the assistant, not visible in the video, collapses the indirect space.
Generally, all gets absorbed anyway. I don't know how much attention to detail is necessary for that.
Again, I take the indirect sack, try to march it up a little bit. I do think this has a little bit less chance of having a recurrence, suturing the flap here.
The surgeon closes down the space at the lead point to prevent the formation of an internal hernia.
